name: Bird Photography Guide for UK birders -  Settings for Birds

step.name: Set up a reliable perch, not just a feeder
step.text: Set up a natural-looking perch (moss-covered branch, dried lavender, ivy) within 30cm of where birds land before the feeder. Rotate every couple of weeks.

step.name: Shoot from indoors through an open window
step.text: Open a kitchen or bedroom window and rest the lens on a beanbag or rolled towel. Garden birds ignore open windows but wary of silhouettes outside.

step.name: Pre-focus and dial in exposure before birds arrive
step.text: Focus on the perch with birds gone, lock exposure on foliage matched for tone. Check shutter is 1/1000s for active birds and aperture covers the bird's depth.

step.name: Use single-point AF on the eye
step.text: Place focus point on the bird's eye, not the body. A sharp eye on slightly soft body reads as a great bird photograph; sharp body with soft eye never does.

step.name: Fire short bursts, not held-down machine guns
step.text: Three-to-five frame bursts at the right moment outperform long held bursts. Watch for head turns, wing-stretch, landing, take-off.

step.name: Watch the background, not just the bird
step.text: Dark fence, distant bush, soft green hedge are ideal; sunlit white wall, shed, clothesline are not. Move perch 60cm to improve background.

step.name: Edit lightly, crop for impact, ditch marginal frames
step.text: Keep 15-20 out of 400 frames. Gentle white balance, contrast, sharpness. Crop for impact. Delete soft-eyed frames, awkward wings, cluttered backgrounds.
